coredata.dc:title: Enhanced the intrinsic oxidase-like activity of chiral carbon dots via manganese doping for selective catalytic oxidation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yan
Zhang, Mengling
Ma, Yurong
Du, Xin
Li, Wenwen
Hu, Tao
Liu, Yang
Huang, Hui
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description:
It is highly desirable to design and construct chemical catalysts with high activity and specificity as the alternatives of natural enzymes for industrial application. Chiral carbon dots (CDs), possessing both the intrinsic enzyme-like activity and specific recognition ability, are one of good candidates for enzyme-like catalysts. However, their catalytic activity is far from that of natural enzymes and needs to be enhanced. In this work, the modulation of the chiral structure and catalytic activity of chiral CDs with intrinsic oxidase-like activity was implemented by manganese (Mn) doping. Under the light condition, chiral CDs (l-Ser-CDs and d-Ser-CDs) derived from chiral serine (Ser) show weak catalytic activity and low selectivity toward the oxidation of L type of dopamine (l-DOPA), whereas the Mn functionalized chiral CDs (l-Mn-CDs or d-Mn-CDs) exhibit 6.9 times higher in catalytic activity and 2.9 times in selectivity ratio (SR) than Ser-CDs. Mn-CDs involve two-path catalytic process, in which the photogenerated electrons could reduce O2 to
O2 − as the active species and the holes would oxidize DOPA directly. Moreover, doping of Mn enables the CDs to generate more
O2 −. Besides, l-Mn-CDs have higher catalytic activity than that of d-Mn-CDs (+54.2 %), and the chiral Mn-CDs have stronger selective adsorption capacity towards chiral DOPA than Ser-CDs. Our work provides a new method for designing and preparing novel chiral artificial enzymes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral carbon dots
Metal doping
Oxidase-like activity
Selective catalytic oxidation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The selective catalytic activity of chiral CDs and Mn-CDs
Preparation and characterization of chiral Mn-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National Key R&D Program of China ( 2020YFA0406104 , 2020YFA0406101 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 52302107 , 52271223 , ‬ 52272043 , 51972216 , 51821002 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BE2022425 , BK20230503 ), Macau Youths Scholars Program ( AM2021008 ), Key R & D program of Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region ( 2022BEG02006 ), Ningxia Autonomous Region flexible introduction of science and technology innovation team ( 2021RXTDLX08 ), The Science and Technology Development Fund, Macau SAR ( 0009/2022/ITP ), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology , the 111 Project , and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and morphology of l -Mn-CDs. (a) Schematic illustration for the preparation of l-Mn-CDs; (b) TEM, (c) HRTEM images and (d) Size distribution of l-Ser-CDs. (e) TEM, (f) Magnified HAADF-STEM images and (g) size distribution of l-Mn-CDs. (h) The magnified HAADF-STEM image of l-Mn-CDs (yellow circles highlight CDs). (i) The magnified HAADF-STEM image of l-Mn-CDs (orange circles highlight single Mn atoms). (j) The structure of seven chains for Mn-CDs (Grey: C; Red: O; Blue: N; White: H; Golden: Mn). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Structural characterizations of l -Mn-CDs. The XPS high resolution (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s and (c) Mn 2p spectra of l-Mn-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectra, (e) UV–Vis, and (f) CD spectra of l-/d-Ser-CDs and l-/d-Mn-CDs. (g) PL spectra of l-Ser-CDs (black line) and l-Mn-CDs (red line). (h) The CV curve of l-Mn-CDs in the N2-saturated tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (0.1 mol/L, dissolved in acetonitrile) with Ag/Ag+ as the reference electrode (the inset shows the diagram of energy band structure of l-Ser-CDs and l-Mn-CDs). (i) The EPR spectra of Ser-CDs and Mn-CDs under dark and visible-light irradiation. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The catalytic activity and stability of l -Mn-CDs towards the oxidation of l-DOPA. (a) The reaction curves and (b) selective catalytic activity of l-Ser-CDs (0.36 mg/mL), MnCl2 (20 μg/mL), and l-Mn-CDs (l-Ser-CDs = 0.36 mg/mL, MnCl2 = 20 μg/mL). (c) Catalytic activity of l-Ser-CDs and l-Mn-CDs with different CDs (Ser-CDs of the complex and the control) concentrations ranging from 0.09 to 0.54 mg/mL. (d) Catalytic activity of MnCl2 and l-Mn-CDs with different Mn2+ concentrations. (e) Lineweaver-Burk Plot of l-Ser-CDs and l-Mn-CDs. (f) Catalytic activity and stability of l-Ser-CDs, and l-Mn-CDs under different pH values. (P < 0.001 is indicated by a triple asterisk (***)). Error estimates represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

The selective catalytic activity of l-/d-Mn-CDs. (a) The reaction curves and (b) selective catalytic activity of l-/d-Mn-CDs (l-/d-Ser-CDs = 0.36 mg/mL, MnCl2 = 20 μg/mL) and l-/d-Ser-CDs (0.36 mg/mL). (c) Catalytic activity of l-/d-Mn-CDs with different Mn2+ concentrations. (d) Catalytic activity of l-/d-Mn-CDs in different concentrations of PB (5 to 30 μg/mL). (e) Selectivity ratios of Mn-CDs in different concentrations of PB (5 to 30 μg/mL). (f) Selectivity ratios of Mn-CDs with different pH values (5, 7, 9, 11). (ns represents no statistical difference, P < 0.05 is indicated by a single asterisk (*), P < 0.001 is indicated by a triple asterisk (***)). Error estimates represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

(a) The product concentrations catalyzed by Ser-CDs, MnCl2 and Mn-CDs under visible-light irradiation and dark conditions. (b) Trapping experiment of active species during the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs. (c) The catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs. (d) The activity difference of MnCl2 and l-Mn-CDs with different Mn2+ concentrations ranging from 5 to 30 μg/mL. (e) The EIS of l-/d-Ser-CDs and l-/d-Mn-CDs in the l-DOPA solution. (f) The transient capacity discharge curve of l-Mn-CDs and d-Mn-CDs in the l-DOPA solution (initial voltage: 0.6 V). Error estimates represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments.
Schematic diagram of catalytic mechanisms of l-Ser-CDs and l-Mn-CDs.

head.ce:title: Enhanced the intrinsic oxidase-like activity of chiral carbon dots via manganese doping for selective catalytic oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering (MIMSE), MUST-SUDA Joint Research Center for Advanced Functional Materials, Macau University of Science and Technology, Taipa 999078, Macao

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering (MIMSE)
MUST-SUDA Joint Research Center for Advanced Functional Materials
Macau University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering (MIMSE), MUST-SUDA Joint Research Center for Advanced Functional Materials, Macau University of Science and Technology, Taipa 999078, Macao

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Mn functionalized chiral CDs (Mn-CDs) exhibit higher oxidase-like catalytic activity and superior selectivity than the undoped Ser-CDs, which are attributed to more
O2 − production, participation of photogenerated holes in the oxidation of DOPA, and the enhanced selective adsorption capacity with substrates.

It is highly desirable to design and construct chemical catalysts with high activity and specificity as the alternatives of natural enzymes for industrial application. Chiral carbon dots (CDs), possessing both the intrinsic enzyme-like activity and specific recognition ability, are one of good candidates for enzyme-like catalysts. However, their catalytic activity is far from that of natural enzymes and needs to be enhanced. In this work, the modulation of the chiral structure and catalytic activity of chiral CDs with intrinsic oxidase-like activity was implemented by manganese (Mn) doping. Under the light condition, chiral CDs (l-Ser-CDs and d-Ser-CDs) derived from chiral serine (Ser) show weak catalytic activity and low selectivity toward the oxidation of L type of dopamine (l-DOPA), whereas the Mn functionalized chiral CDs (l-Mn-CDs or d-Mn-CDs) exhibit 6.9 times higher in catalytic activity and 2.9 times in selectivity ratio (SR) than Ser-CDs. Mn-CDs involve two-path catalytic process, in which the photogenerated electrons could reduce O2 to
O2 − as the active species and the holes would oxidize DOPA directly. Moreover, doping of Mn enables the CDs to generate more
O2 −. Besides, l-Mn-CDs have higher catalytic activity than that of d-Mn-CDs (+54.2 %), and the chiral Mn-CDs have stronger selective adsorption capacity towards chiral DOPA than Ser-CDs. Our work provides a new method for designing and preparing novel chiral artificial enzymes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural enzymes, as powerful biocatalysts, have been widely applied in industrial, medical, and biological fields [1]. However, the inherent drawbacks of natural enzymes, such as low stability and high costs of preparation and purification, seriously limit their practical applications [2,3]. To overcome these drawbacks, various nanomaterials have received considerable attention due to its stable structures, low costs, and adjustable activities [4–7]. Up till now, traditional nanomaterials are mainly constructed by simulating the pivotal structural features of natural enzymes, such as the amino acid microenvironment, the metal-free architecture, and the coordination structure of active center and attached cofactors [8,9]. However, the strategies for the design of related catalysts are still lacking for these artificial enzymes without well-defined nanostructure, which severely hinder their further development for biomedical applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) have become a research hotspot in biomedical fields owing to their good water solubility, biocompatibility and abundant functional groups [10–12]. Significantly, it has been discovered that CDs have multifarious enzyme activities including peroxidase-like activity, oxidase-like activity, superoxide dismutase-like activity, and so on [13–17]. In recent years, CDs have been widely used as oxidase-like enzymes in the fields of antioxidation, anti-tumor and antimicrobial resistance [18], but the enzyme-like activity of currently developed CDs is still very low [19–22]. Noble metals and their alloys with outstanding oxygen reduction catalytic activities are universally acknowledged as oxidase mimics. However, the industrialization of noble metal materials is limited by their high price, scarcity, and unavailability. Various noble metals and their alloys (Pt, Au, Ag. e.g.) have been investigated as oxidase mimics, given their exceptional catalytic activities in oxygen reduction [23–27]. However, their widespread applications are hampered by high prices and unavailability. Therefore, there is an urgent need to enhance the activity and selectivity of CDs as oxidase-like nanomaterials through modification or other means.

In the previous work, we reported the synthesis of twin chiral CDs (l-Ser-CDs or d-Ser-CDs) with clear primary and spatial structure from serine enantiomers (l-Ser or d-Ser), along with their weak enantioselective catalytic activity for the oxidation of DOPA enantiomers [28]. Herein, chiral CDs with oxidase-like catalytic activity were prepared by loading with Mn to further improve the catalytic performance. Chiral CDs were synthesized through electrochemical method from serine (Ser), and the Mn functionalized CDs (l-Mn-CDs or d-Mn-CDs) were obtained by stirring Ser-CDs with MnCl2 solution. The morphology, structure, chirality, optical properties, and band structure of the obtained one pair of chiral CDs were compared, and their catalytic activity and selectivity were analyzed. In addition, the roles of the Mn and the catalytic mechanism of Mn-CDs were demonstrated. This work provides a promising strategy to design chiral artificial enzymes with high catalytic activity and selectivity.

l-Ser and d-Ser (99 %) were provided by Sigma Aldrich (Shanghai) Trading Co., Ltd. Dopamine (DOPA) was purchased from Shanghai Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. NaOH and MnCl2 (99 %) were purchased from Sinopharm chemical reagent Co., Ltd. Cellulose dialysis bag (500–1000 Da) was obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co. Ltd. Graphite rod (99.99 %) was purchased from Shanghai Carbon Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used without any further purification.

Chiral CDs were prepared by one-step electrochemical oxidation method as previous report by our group [28]. Typically, 3.00 g of l-/d-Ser and 1.2 g of NaOH were sufficiently dissolved in 300 mL of ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm). Then, two graphite rods were inserted into the above solution as anode and cathode, respectively. The constant voltage of 3.00 V was supplied by a DC power supply. After 72 h of electrolysis, the products were purified through 500–1000 Da dialysis bag for 3–5 days. Finally, chiral CDs (l-Ser-CDs and d-Ser-CDs) were obtained.

Different concentrations of MnCl2 solution (0.25, 0.5, 1 and 1.5 mg/mL) were added into the obtained chiral CDs (l-Ser-CDs or d-Ser-CDs: 1 mg/mL). The concentrations of MnCl2 in these compounds are 5, 10, 20 and 30 μg/mL. Then the mixtures were stirred at room temperature for 12 h. To remove the remaining MnCl2, ultrafiltration was conducted using 500–1000 Da dialysis bag. Finally, the chiral Mn-CDs (l-Mn-CDs and d-Mn-CDs) were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selective catalytic activity of chiral CDs and Mn-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activities of chiral CDs and Mn-CDs were measured via colorimetric method for monitoring the oxidation of dopamine (DOPA). The reaction system: chiral CDs or Mn-CDs with different concentrations, DOPA (4 mM) and phosphate buffer (PB, 2 mM, pH = 7.0). Then, the reaction system was incubated at 25 °C under visible-light irradiation of 4.80 mW/cm2. For comparison, the catalytic activity of MnCl2 (20 μg/mL) was measured as well. Besides, the effects of pH (3–11), temperature (4–45 °C), ionic strengths (0–1 M) and time (0–60 days) on the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs or d-Mn-CDs were examined for evaluating the stability of Mn-CDs.

Active species capture experiments were performed for analyzing the active substances during the catalytic process. 1 mM tetraacetic acid (EDTA), 10 mM CuCl2, 1 mM terephthalic acid (TPA), 0.2 mg/mL superoxide dismutase (SOD), and 10 mM tryptophan (Try) were added into the reaction system to detect the hole (h+), electron (e−),
OH,
O2 −, and 1O2, respectively. Then, the absorbance of the reaction system was detected by UV–Vis absorption spectrum (475 nm), and the catalytic activity was calculated.

Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurement was utilized to analyze the generating ability of Mn-CDs for
O2 −. DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide) was used as the capture agents in this measurement. 10 mg of CDs was dispersed in 10 mL methanol (1 mg/mL). 10 mL of the above solution was taken out and mixed with 10 mL of DMPO. The EPR spectrum was captured under dark and visible-light irradiation condition (5 min).

The electrochemical test was performed on an electrochemical analyzer (CHI 760E, CH Instruments, Shanghai, China) in a standard three–electrode system. The chiral CDs or Mn-CDs modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE), carbon electrode and Ag/AgCl electrodes were utilized as the working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The loading mass of CDs on GCE is about 2 mg/cm2 (0.5 wt. % Nafion, as the solvent). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test of all samples were carried out at open circuit voltage, with the frequency ranging from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz and the modulation amplitude of 5 mV. All the measurements were carried out at room temperature.

The transient capacity discharge measurement was carried out by a self-designed capacitor discharge setup. In this work, the transient capacity discharge curves were measured in N2-saturated PB solution containing l-DOPA (DOPA: 4 mM; PB: 2 mM). Mn-CDs or CDs-GCE was used as working electrode and Pt wire was adopted as counter electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of chiral Mn-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the following description, l-Mn-CDs were described as an example for the chiral CDs. The synthetic process of the chiral Mn-CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 a. The precursor Ser-CDs (l-Ser-CDs or d-Ser-CDs) were obtained by electrochemical polymerization from l-Ser or d-Ser under alkaline conditions as the previous report by our group [28]. Then, Ser-CDs were mixed with MnCl2 at room temperature for Mn doping, and finally the chiral Mn-CDs were acquired. Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) images (Fig. 1b-1e) show that both l-Ser-CDs and l-Mn-CDs are nearly spherical and have good dispersibility. After complexing with the Mn2+, the size distribution shows that the average particle size of l-Mn-CDs is 4.5 nm, while the average size of l-Ser-CDs is 4.0 nm. Aberration-corrected high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) was employed to verify the presence of atomically dispersed Mn (Fig. 1f). As displayed in Fig. 1h-1i, the dispersive Mn atoms are substantiated on the CDs, in which CDs and the single Mn atoms are marked with yellow and orange circles, respectively. The TEM and HAADF-STEM images of d-Ser-CDs and d-Mn-CDs are displayed in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2. Moreover, determined by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), the content of Mn in Mn-CDs is 26 μg/mL (Table S1). Based on the morphology and the structure of Ser-CDs reported in previous work [28], Ser-CDs consist of multiple polycyclic dipeptide chains (Fig. S3). The structure diagram of seven chains for Mn-CDs is displayed in Fig. 1j, where the Mn atoms are dispersed on the chains of the Ser-CDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze elemental composition and chemical structure of samples. The C 1s spectrum of l-Mn-CDs (Fig. 2 a) shows that the peaks are assigned to CO (284.6 eV), CO/CN (286.1 eV), and CC (288.4 eV) [29]. The peaks at 399.8 and 401.4 eV in N 1s spectrum could be assigned to pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. 2b) [30]. The partial spectra of C 1s and N 1s demonstrate there are abundant functional groups on the surfaces of Mn-CDs. Moreover, given the Mn species existed in a monatomic dispersed form, the signal of Mn 2p is weak. Fig. 2c reveals Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 at 653.28 and 640.18 eV, respectively, demonstrating the valence state of Mn atom is 2 [31,32]. The elemental compositions and chemical structure of d-Mn-CDs (Fig. S4) are similar with l-Mn-CDs.

To further analyze the structure and composition of Mn-CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was also carried out. The FT-IR spectrum for l-Mn-CDs and l-Ser-CDs are presented in Fig. 2d. With regard to l-Mn-CDs, four prominent peaks were observed. The absorption peak at 3446 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH of carboxyl/amidogen groups. The peaks at 1631 cm−1 and 1040 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CO, respectively. Besides, the peak is located at 1382 cm−1, which could be ascribed to the stretching of the CN bond [33,34]. Importantly, no obvious characteristic peak of MnO could be observed in the FT-IR spectrum of Mn-CDs due to the low content of Mn. In addition, Mn-CDs exhibit similar functional groups compared with Ser-CDs, and the L type and D type Mn-CDs (Fig. S5) show similar FT-IR results.

The optical properties and chirality of CDs were characterized by the UV–Vis absorption and the circular dichroism (CD) spectra. In the UV–Vis spectrum of l-Ser-CDs (Fig. 2e, black line), distinct peaks were observed at 204 and 298 nm, which correspond to electron transitions of π-π* and n-π*, respectively [35,36]. l-Mn-CDs exhibited similar light absorption characteristics to l-Ser-CDs, which was consistent with the UV–Vis absorption spectra of d-Ser-CDs and d-Mn-CDs (Fig. S6). Fig. 2f is the CD spectra of the chiral Ser-CDs and Mn-CDs (MnCl2: 20 μg/mL). The chiral Ser-CDs exhibit symmetric CD curves, with their chiral signal located at 218 nm. After Mn doping, Mn-CDs not only maintain the chiral signal at 218 nm from the Ser-CDs but also produce a new and faint CD band with a peak at 300 nm. Fig. S7 is the CD spectra of Mn-CDs with different Mn content. The newly generated chiral signal is enhanced with increasing Mn content in Mn-CDs, indicating the influence of Mn doping on the chirality of Ser-CDs. Moreover, the fluorescence emission (PL) and excitation (PLE) spectra of Ser-CDs and Mn-CDs were measured to further characterize their optical properties. The l-Ser-CDs (0.36 mg/mL, Fig. 2g) reveal an optimal emission at 432 nm with the excitation of 344 nm (Fig. S8), while the fluorescence intensity of the l-Mn-CDs with the same concentration decreases significantly. This suggests that the doping of Mn may change their surface state of Ser-CDs, leading to the reduction of fluorescence intensity. The PL and PLE spectra of D type CDs are displayed in Fig. S9.

Next, the energy band structures of Ser-CDs and Mn-CDs were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) [37]. In Fig. 2h and Fig. S10, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) were calculated using the formulae provided in the ESI. The HOMO and LUMO of Ser-CDs were calculated to be 1.58 and −1.20 V (vs RHE), respectively. For Mn-CDs, the HOMO and LUMO were calculated to be 1.58 and −1.19 V (vs RHE), respectively. According to schematic diagram of the energy band structure (inset of Fig. 2h), the LUMO potentials of Ser-CDs and Mn-CDs are negative than the reduction potential of O2/
O2 − (-0.33 V vs RHE) [38], indicating their ability to reduce O2 and produce
O2 − [39]. Moreover, the EPR spectra of l-Mn-CDs and l-Ser-CDs were measured under dark and visible-light irradiation conditions. As shown in Fig. 2i, l-Mn-CDs exhibit clear superoxide radical signals (1:1:1) under both conditions, confirming that l-Mn-CDs can produce
O2 − in both dark and visible-light irradiation. However, for l-Ser-CDs, a weak signal for
O2 − is observed under visible-light irradiation, and no EPR signal could be found under dark condition. Moreover, l-Mn-CDs show higher superoxide radical signals than l-Ser-CDs under the same treatment of light condition, implying that l-Mn-CDs can produce more
O2 −. Based on the above discussion, it could be concluded that the doping of Mn in Ser-CDs does not change the morphology of the CDs, but would affect their chirality and surface state, thereby exhibiting different CD signals and fluorescence intensity. Moreover, the CV measurement and EPR test prove that l-Mn-CDs have the activity of oxygen reduction under the dark and visible-light irradiation conditions.

In our previous research, we found that the chiral CDs derived from Ser show enantioselective catalytic activity towards the oxidation of DOPA. Next, the catalytic activity of composite and pristine materials was investigated through catalytic oxidation of l-DOPA under visible-light irradiation (4.80 mW/cm2). Following the oxidation, the UV–Vis spectra of l-Ser-CDs/DOPA, MnCl2/DOPA and l-Mn-CDs/DOPA systems were recorded, and their catalytic activities were calculated. Fig. 3 a and Fig. S11 show the different catalytic performances of the composites and pristine materials toward DOPA. l-Ser-CDs and MnCl2 possess weak catalytic activity, whereas the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs significantly increases. Meanwhile, Fig. 3b displays that compared with l-Ser-CDs and MnCl2, the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs markedly increases 571.1 % and 499.3 %, respectively. These results indicate that Mn-CDs possess intrinsic oxidase-like activity and their catalytic activity surpasses that of the individual components. Then, the reaction conditions, such as Ser-CDs concentrations and MnCl2 content, were further optimized. As depicted in Fig. 3c, the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs and l-Ser-CDs increases gradually with the increase of Ser-CDs concentrations from 0.09 to 0.54 mg/mL. Moreover, the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs is positively related to the MnCl2 content when the Ser-CDs concentration is 0.36 mg/mL. As shown in Fig. 3d, the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs with different MnCl2 contents increase obviously, implying that the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs is concentration-dependent.

From the Lineweavere-Burk double reciprocal plots, the kinetic parameters including the values of maximum initial velocity (Vmax) and Michaelise-Menten constant (Km) were obtained in Fig. 3e and Fig. S12. The Km value of l-Mn-CDs (1.99 × 10−3 mol/L) is significantly lower than that of d-Mn-CDs (3.04 × 10−3 mol/L), indicating that l-Mn-CDs have an extremely excellent affinity with l-DOPA, which may be the main reason for its high catalytic activity. Although the Vmax of d-Mn-CDs is higher than that of l-Mn-CDs, the high Km value of d-Mn-CDs makes its catalytic ability inferior to the latter. We also calculated the Km values of l-Ser-CDs and d-Ser-CDs. The results show that the Km value is 5.61 × 10−3 mol/L and 6.93 × 10−3 mol/L. Both the chiral Mn-CDs demonstrate lower Km values than those of the chiral Ser-CDs.

To explore the stability of the catalytic activity of the obtained Mn-CDs, the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs and d-Mn-CDs were tested at different pH (3–11), temperatures (4–45 °C), ionic strengths (0–1 M) and time (0–60 days). In Fig. 3f, it can be seen that l-Mn-CDs and d-Mn-CDs maintain high catalytic activity under alkaline conditions (pH = 11), implying that alkaline conditions can accelerate the oxidation reaction. However, the oxidation reaction hardly occurs under acidic conditions (pH = 3). When the pH is maintained around neutral, the catalytic activities of l-Mn-CDs and d-Mn-CDs remain stable to a certain extent. Furthermore, Fig. S13a displays that the catalytic activities of l-Mn-CDs and d-Mn-CDs enhance temperatures increase, indicating that temperature plays a critical role in the catalytic process. As shown in Fig. S13b, the chiral Mn-CDs can still maintain stable catalytic activity under high ionic strength, indicating that they are more stable than natural enzymes. Additionally, Fig. S13c depicts the effects of time on the catalytic activity of CDs. As the extend of storage time, the catalytic activity of chiral Mn-CDs remains stable. These results confirm that Mn-CDs possess good stability and can exhibit higher catalytic activity in alkaline and high-temperature solutions compared with natural enzymes.

According to the previous study demonstrating Ser-CDs possess selective oxidase-like activity, the selectivity of Mn-CDs was also explored. For further evaluating the selectivity of Mn-CDs, selectivity ratio (SR) was defined as the ratio of the difference in catalytic activity between l-type and d-type Mn-CDs (△Mn) to the difference in catalytic activity between l-type and d-type Ser-CDs (△Ser), denoted as SR=(△Mn)/(△Ser). A larger SR value represents higher difference between Mn-CDs and Ser-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 a and Fig. S14, the reaction curves indicate that the relatively low activity of Ser-CDs in DOPA oxidation, is significantly lower than that of Mn-CDs. Fig. 4b shows that the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs (l-Ser-CDs) increases by 54.2 % (18.5 %) compared with d-Mn-CDs (d-Ser-CDs). The value of SR is 2.9, indicating that the selectivity of Mn-CDs is larger than that of Ser-CDs. Meanwhile, the catalytic activity of Mn-CDs with different Mn contents was measured. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the catalytic activity increases gradually with the increase of Mn content in Mn-CDs (5–30 μg/mL). However, the selectivity of Mn-CDs displays the highest catalytic selectivity (+54.2 %) when the MnCl2 concentration is 20 μg/mL. Besides, Mn-CDs show a higher selectivity with an enhanced concentration of PB (Fig. 4d), whereas their catalytic activity significantly decreases. Besides, the SR of the Mn-CDs gradually enhances with the increase of PB concentration. Meanwhile, the effect of pH on the selectivity of Mn-CDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4f, Mn-CDs exhibit a higher SR (2.9) in the pH of 7.

The catalytic activities of Ser-CDs and Mn-CDs were also conducted under visible-light irradiation and dark condition. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs is much higher than that of l-Ser-CDs or MnCl2 under both conditions. For Ser-CDs, they only have catalytic activity in the condition of visible-light irradiation (4.80 mW/cm2). But for Mn-CDs, they could exhibit high catalytic activity under dark condition, and their catalytic activities significantly increase with the participation of visible light.

Fig. 5b displays that the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs in the presence of different sacrificial agents (tetraacetic acid (EDTA)/h+, CuCl2/e−, terephthalic acid (TPA)/
OH, superoxide dismutase (SOD)/
O2 −, and tryptophan (Try)/1O2) [21]. It can be seen that e−, h+ and
O2 − take roles in the catalytic process. The addition of CuCl2 inhibits the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs (50.3 %), while EDTA significantly reduces the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs (70.4 %), implying that the decrease of electrons and holes inhibits the reaction. Moreover, SOD has a stronger inhibitory effect on the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs (37.8 %) compared to TPA (0.22 %) and Try (20.8 %), illustrating that
O2 − is the main active species for the catalytic activity of L-Mn-CDs.

As depicted in Fig. 5c, with the increase of Mn content in Mn-CDs (5–30 μg/mL), the catalytic activity of l-Mn-CDs increases rapidly below 20 μg/mL, and then changes little when their concentration is increased to 30 μg/mL. Moreover, when the Mn content is 20 μg/mL, the selectivity of l-Mn-CDs is the highest (+54.2 %, Fig. 5d). Therefore, the doping of Mn can effectively improve the activity and selectivity of CDs towards the oxidation of DOPA by modulating the electron transport ability, chirality and free radical generation ability of the CDs.

The adsorption capacity of the samples was further evaluated using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As shown in Fig. 5e, the semicircle radius of the EIS test for l-Ser-CDs modified GCE is significantly larger than that of d-Ser-CDs modified GCE in l-DOPA, indicating that l-Ser-CDs possess stronger adsorption than d-Ser-CDs on l-DOPA. The selectivity of the chiral Mn-CDs towards DOPA could be confirmed by EIS. Compared to the EIS of the d-Mn-CDs on DOPA, l-Mn-CDs have much larger semicircle radius, revealing that DOPA is more likely to adsorb on l-Mn-CDs than d-Mn-CDs. Thus, the selective catalytic activity of chiral CDs would be due to the selective absorption of the chiral CDs toward DOPA.

Transient capacitance discharge test was used to analyze the storage and consumption behavior of charge on the catalyst interface. To prevent the oxidation of DOPA, the transient capacitance discharge test was detected in the N2 atmosphere, with an initial voltage set below 0.6 V, which is lower than the oxidation peak of DOPA. Fig. 5f displays the current decay curves of l-Mn-CDs and d-Mn-CDs. The time for the charge consumption (τ) could be obtained by fitting the current decay curves (fit curves in Fig. S15). The τ value can represent the degree of adsorption between catalyst and substrates. As shown in Fig. 5f, l-Mn-CDs (τL = 10.9 ms) exhibit a higher τ value than d-Mn-CDs (τD = 7.9 ms). The results of transient capacitance discharge test indicate that l-Mn-CDs have stronger adsorption towards DOPA than d-Mn-CDs as well, which may be the reason of the selective catalytic activity of chiral Mn-CDs.

Based on the above discussion, the mechanism of Mn-CDs for the catalytic oxidation of DOPA was speculated (shown in Fig. 6 ). The addition of Mn would increase the oxidase-like activity and selectivity of chiral Ser-CDs. The catalytic activity of Ser-CDs is derived from the reduction of O2 to
O2 − by CDs photogenerated electrons, whereas both the photogenerated electrons and holes of Mn-CDs participate in the oxidation of DOPA. Specifically, for Mn-CDs, the photogenerated electrons could reduce O2 to
O2 − as the active O species for the DOPA oxidation and the holes would oxidize DOPA directly. Moreover, the doping of Mn enables the CDs to generate
O2 − under dark condition, so that the Mn-CDs have catalytic activity in the dark as well. Besides, the doping of Mn makes Mn-CDs have stronger selective adsorption capacity for DOPA compared with Ser-CDs, and exhibit higher selectivity towards the catalytic oxidation of chiral DOPA.

In summary, we modulated the chiral structure and catalytic activity of chiral CDs with intrinsic oxidase-like activity via Mn doping. Currently, metal ions doping CDs have been found to exhibit high enzyme-like activity, but the selectivity of CDs is considerably poor [5,14,21,28,40]. This work has synthesized Mn-CDs that exhibit higher oxidase-like activity and selectivity compared to undoped Ser-CDs. Using catalytic oxidation of DOPA as the model reaction, the Mn functionalized chiral CDs (l-Mn-CDs or d-Mn-CDs) exhibit superior oxidase-like activity (6.9 times) than undoped Ser-CDs. The superior catalytic activity of Mn-CDs is due to the different catalytic mechanisms. For Ser-CDs,
O2 − generated by photogenerated electrons is the mainly active species. For Mn-CDs, both the photogenerated electrons and holes of Mn-CDs participate in the oxidation of DOPA. The photogenerated electrons could reduce O2 to
O2 − as the active species, and the photogenerated holes would oxidize DOPA directly. Moreover, Mn-CDs have higher catalytic activity than the undoped CDs, and Mn-CDs still possess catalytic activity even in dark condition due to the doping of Mn enabling the CDs to generate more
O2 −. Additionally, Mn-CDs exhibit higher selectivity (SR: 2.9 times higher) towards the catalytic oxidation of chiral DOPA than Ser-CDs because the addition of Mn lets the Mn-CDs have stronger selective adsorption capacity to the substrate of l-DOPA than Ser-CDs. This work offers insights into the rational design of efficient stereoselective artificial enzymes as well as the roles of metal in the oxidase-like catalytic process of metal doped chiral carbon nanomaterials.

Yan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis. Mengling Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Yurong Ma: Investigation. Xin Du: Formal analysis. Wenwen Li: Visualization. Tao Hu: Methodology. Yang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis. Hui Huang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhenhui Kang: Funding acquisition.
